pages.text: In the quiet village of Sundargram, the stars twinkled like tiny diyas every night. But one evening, when <name> looked up from her courtyard, she gasped. The sky was dark — no stars, no moon, only silent clouds drifting above.

“What happened to the stars, Amma?” she asked. Amma smiled. “Maybe they’re just resting, beta.” But <name> wasn’t so sure. Later that night, as she sat near the mango tree, she saw a soft glow floating down from the sky — a golden lantern! It landed right beside her feet. The light shimmered and whispered, “Follow me.”

Curious, <name> tiptoed through the fields until she reached the top of the hill. There, hidden behind the mist, was a cave made of glowing crystals. Inside, she saw a dragon — not scary at all, but gentle and shining with rainbow-colored scales.

“Ah, you found my lantern!” boomed the dragon in a warm, rumbling voice. “I’m Dhuma, keeper of the winds and stars.” <name> bowed politely. “I’m <name>. Why is the sky dark tonight?” Dhuma sighed. “My lantern’s flame went out while I was sleeping. Without it, the stars cannot shine.”

<name> thought hard. “Maybe we can relight it with something from the earth!” She opened her satchel and took out a diya from the Diwali shelf at home. “This light is for hope,” she said softly. She placed the diya under the lantern — flicker… flicker… WHOOSH! The lantern glowed bright gold, and the dragon’s scales shimmered like jewels.

“The stars are waking up!” cried <name>. Outside, the sky began to sparkle again, one star at a time. Dhuma bowed his glittering head. “Thank you, brave one. You’ve reminded me that even the smallest light can bring back the stars.”

<name> smiled. “And friendship can make it shine even brighter!” The dragon laughed softly, his voice like thunder wrapped in a lullaby. “Come back anytime, little light-bringer.” As she walked home under the glowing sky, <name> looked up and saw one special star twinkling extra bright — shaped just like a tiny lantern.
